statement.markdown: Implement the function `modularArithmetic(a, m)` that takes two integers `a` and `m` and returns the result of `a` modulo `m` in O(1) time complexity. The function should work correctly for negative numbers and edge cases.
statement.outputFormat: The integer result of `a` modulo `m`. If `m` is 0, return -1.
statement.notes: Use bitwise operations to achieve O(1) time complexity.

hints.text: Use bitwise operations to handle negative numbers and edge cases.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The key idea is to use bitwise operations to handle negative numbers and edge cases.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: use the built-in modulus operator.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use bitwise operations to handle negative numbers and edge cases.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The optimal solution is correct because it handles all edge cases and has a time complexity of O(1).

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: int modularArithmetic(int a, int m) { return a >= 0 ? a % m : a; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public int modularArithmetic(int a, int m) { return a >= 0 ? a % m : a; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def modularArithmetic(self, a: int, m: int) -> int: return a >= 0 and a % m or a
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function modularArithmetic(a, m) { return a >= 0 ? a % m : a; }
